skill.name: hig-components-layout
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines reference for layout and navigation components, including sidebars, split views, tab bars, scroll views, windows, panels, lists, tables, column views, outline views, boxes, and visionOS ornaments.
skill.summary: Design adaptive Apple app layouts using HIG guidance for navigation, hierarchy, windows, and platform-specific layout behavior.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but evaluator review found documentation-only Markdown content. Backtick detections are inline Apple API names, URLs point to Apple Developer documentation or Apple-hosted images, and crypto or C2 matches are spurious text or hash-like asset identifiers. No executable code, command invocation, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or unsafe network behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Inline API References Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown links that display Apple API names in backticks, such as SwiftUI, UIKit, and AppKit component names. They are documentation references and do not execute shell commands or Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown text and links to Apple API documentation. No executable script, shell syntax, or command runner is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Official Documentation URLs Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The network detections are hardcoded links to Apple Developer documentation, Apple documentation assets, and the author site in static Markdown. The skill does not contain code that opens these URLs or sends data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in Markdown source and image links only. I found no fetch, curl, webhook, or programmatic network request pattern in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hash-Like Apple Asset Identifiers Flagged as Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections correspond to hash-like strings inside Apple-hosted image asset URLs and documentation anchors. They are resource identifiers, not calls to MD5, SHA1, or other cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to static Markdown URLs containing opaque asset IDs. There is no cryptographic API usage or password handling context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: UI Design Terms Flagged as C2 or Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: C2 and reconnaissance hits are ordinary Apple HIG language about controls, commands, files, windows, and navigation. The content describes interface behavior and contains no malware control channel, host discovery, or system inspection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched terms are embedded in user interface documentation. No evidence of command-and-control behavior, reconnaissance commands, or malicious intent was found.

content.value_statement: Apple apps need layouts that fit each platform and window size. This skill gives concise HIG guidance for navigation, hierarchy, windows, sidebars, tabs, and spatial layouts.
actual_capabilities: Recommends sidebars, tab bars, split views, or column views based on app hierarchy.
Explains platform differences for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and visionOS layout components.
Summarizes HIG guidance for windows, panels, scroll views, lists, tables, boxes, and ornaments.
Points to relevant SwiftUI, UIKit, AppKit, WatchKit, and QuickLook component documentation.
Provides adaptation checks for compact width, regular width, iPad multitasking, orientation, and accessibility.
Helps compare navigation patterns for shallow sections, deep hierarchies, inspectors, and spatial tools.
limitations: It is a documentation reference and does not implement app code automatically.
It focuses on Apple platform layout guidance, not Android or web design systems.
It summarizes local reference files and may not include every current Apple documentation update.
It does not replace testing in Xcode, Simulator, real devices, or Apple Vision Pro.

use_cases.title: Choose an iPad Navigation Pattern
use_cases.description: A developer compares tab bars, sidebars, and split views before building an adaptive iPad layout.

use_cases.title: Plan a Cross-Platform App Layout
use_cases.description: A product designer maps one information architecture across iPhone, iPad, Mac, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.

use_cases.title: Review Spatial Interface Structure
use_cases.description: A visionOS team checks when to use windows, volumes, ornaments, sidebars, and tab bars.

prompt_templates.title: Select a Basic Navigation Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new app structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have four top-level sections: Home, Search, Library, and Settings. Which Apple navigation pattern should I use on iPhone and iPad?

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a Layout Across Devices
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request about responsive Apple layouts.
prompt_templates.prompt: My iPad app uses three columns, but it breaks on iPhone. Explain the HIG adaptation approach for compact and regular widths.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Component Choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare sidebars, tab bars, split views, and column views for a file-based productivity app across iPad and Mac.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Multi-Platform Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before implementation or release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this layout plan against Apple HIG: document window, sidebar, inspector panel, scrollable detail area, and visionOS ornament controls.

output_examples.input: Should my document editor use a sidebar or an inspector panel?
output_examples.output: Use a sidebar for primary navigation between documents or sections. Use an inspector panel for properties, formatting, and controls related to the current selection.

output_examples.input: My app has six top-level areas. Is a tab bar still the best choice?
output_examples.output: A tab bar works best for a small set of peer sections. With six areas, consider grouping sections or using an adaptive sidebar on larger screens.

output_examples.input: Where should persistent controls live in a visionOS window?
output_examples.output: Use ornaments for persistent controls that should stay near the window without covering content. Keep them balanced and avoid crowding window edges.

best_practices: Start with the app information hierarchy before choosing a navigation component.
Test compact width, regular width, iPad multitasking, orientation changes, and accessibility sizes.
Use system components when possible because they provide platform behavior, adaptation, and accessibility support.
anti_patterns: Choosing a sidebar for shallow iPhone navigation that would work better as a tab bar.
Using fixed layouts that fail when iPad windows resize or enter multitasking modes.
Adding custom window, tab, or scroll behavior when the system component already solves the need.

faq.question: When should I use a tab bar instead of a sidebar?
faq.answer: Use a tab bar for a small set of peer top-level sections. Use a sidebar for broader or deeper navigation, especially on iPad and Mac.

faq.question: Does this skill write SwiftUI or UIKit code?
faq.answer: It provides design guidance and references relevant APIs. It does not generate a full implementation by itself.

faq.question: Which platforms does the guidance cover?
faq.answer: The references cover iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and visionOS where each component is supported.

faq.question: How does it help with iPad multitasking?
faq.answer: It highlights size changes, split ratios, window resizing, and layout collapse behavior that should be tested on iPad.

faq.question: What are ornaments in visionOS?
faq.answer: Ornaments are controls or information attached near a window. They keep tools accessible without covering the main content.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible skill workflows.
